figures.caption: Table 3: ACEBench accuracy on Qwen2.5-Instruct-7B/14B. All numbers are accuracies (%).
Table 2: BFCL v3 accuracy based on Qwen2.5-Instruct-7B/14B, with TOUCAN-SFT or COVERT-RL. All numbers are accuracies (%). Bold for the best performance.
Table 4: Performance on general-ability benchmarks: LiveCodeBench-v5, AIME 2024, AIME 2025, Math500, IFEval (Prompt-Strict), GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro, and GSM8K.
Figure 5: Training reward curves for COVERT-RL initialized from Qwen2.5-Instruct (blue) and from TOUCAN-SFT (orange), at 7B (left) and 14B (right) scales.
Table 7: Full BFCL v3 accuracy for all Qwen2.5-Instruct-7B/14B variants (extends Table 5). All numbers are accuracies (%).
Table 8: Full ACEBench accuracy for all Qwen2.5-Instruct-7B/14B variants (extends Table 5). All numbers are accuracies (%).

Figure 1: Overview of the proposed COVERT pipeline. Stage 1 generates reliable base tool-use trajectories with simple queries and single tools via self-evolution and validation. Stage 2 applies oracle-preserving augmentations to produce complex multi-tool scenarios with noisy and multi-format outputs, while preserving oracle tool behaviors for reward computation during RL training.
Figure 2: Reliable tool-use trajectory generation pipeline (Stage I) with diverse prompt construction, LLM-based generation, multi-level trajectory checking, and self-evolving seed pool expansion.
Figure 8: Examples 3: Raw tool-calling data example of multi-turn tool calling.
Figure 4: System prompt used for evaluation.
Figure 9: Raw case-study conversations (Case 1) comparing base vs. COVERT-RL models.
Figure 7: Examples 2: Raw tool-calling data example of parallel tool calling.
Table 5: Summary of overall accuracy on BFCL v3 and ACEBench for all Qwen2.5-Instruct7B/14B variants.
Figure 10: Raw case-study conversations (Case 2) comparing base vs. COVERT-RL models.
Table 1: Augmentation families in Stage II. “Modified”: perturbed component; “Oracle preserved”: fields for reward verification; “Knobs”: controllable parameters; “Verifier”: exact or judge-assisted.

Figure 3: Illustration of oracle-preserving augmentation. Starting from a simple and reliable base tool-calling trajectory (left), we apply controlled perturbations to the user query and tool environment (right), including indirect entity references, distractor tools, and injected tool-call failures.
Figure 6: Examples 1: Raw tool-calling data examples of layered symbolic reasoning.
